concepts.title: Resemblance with Alkali Metals
concepts.conceptBody:
Resemblance with Alkali Metals

1. Electronic configuration

Hydrogen contains one electron in the valence shell like alkali metals

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \mathbf{H} & : & 1~{{\text{s}}^{1}} \\ \mathbf{Li} & : & [\text{He}]2~{{\text{s}}^{1}} \\ \mathbf{Na} & : & [\text{Ne}]3~{{\text{s}}^{1}} \\ \mathbf{K} & : & [\text{Ar}]4~{{\text{s}}^{1}} \\ \mathbf{Rb} & : & [\text{Kr}]5~{{\text{s}}^{1}} \\ \end{array}$

2. Electropositive character

Like alkali metal, hydrogen also loses its only electron to form hydrogen ion, i.e., ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$

$\underset{1~{{\text{s}}^{1}}}{\mathop{\text{H}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{1~{{\text{s}}^{0}}}{\mathop{{{\text{H}}^{+}}}}\,+{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

$\underset{1~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2{{\text{p}}^{6}}3~{{\text{s}}^{1}}}{\mathop{\text{Na}}}\,\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{1~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2{{\text{p}}^{6}}}{\mathop{\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}}}\,+{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

3. Oxidation state

Like alkali metals, hydrogen exhibits an oxidation state of +1 in its compounds.

$\begin{matrix} {{\text{H}}^{+}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}} & \text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}} & {{\text{K}}^{+}}\text{B}{{\text{r}}^{-}} \\ \text{Hydrogen chloride } & \text{ Sodium chloride } & \text{ Potassium chloride } \\ \end{matrix}$

4. Reducing agent

Alkali metals act as reducing agents because of their tendency to lose valence electron. Hydrogen is also a very good reducing agent as evident from the following reactions:

$\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{3}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }3\text{Fe}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O }$

$\text{CuO}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{Cu}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

5. Combination with electronegative elements

Just like alkali metals hydrogen combines with electronegative elements such as halogen, oxygen, sulphur, etc to form compounds with similar formulae

$\begin{matrix} \text{ Halides } & \text{ Oxides } & \text{ Sulphides } \\ \text{HCl} & {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} & {{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S} \\ \text{NaCl} & \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{O} & \text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}~\text{S} \\ \end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Resemblance with Halogens
concepts.conceptBody:
Resemblance with Halogens

1. Electronic configuration

Just like halogens, hydrogen needs one electron to attain the configuration of nearest noble gas.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{H} & : & 1~{{\text{s}}^{1}} \\ ~\text{F} & : & 1~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2{{\text{p}}^{5}} \\  \text{Cl} & : & 1~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2~{{\text{s}}^{2}}2{{\text{p}}^{6}}3~{{\text{s}}^{2}}{{_{3\text{p}}}^{5}} \\ \end{array}$

2. Atomicity

Like halogens, hydrogen also exists in a diatomic state. The atomicity of hydrogen as well as halogens is two.

3. Electrochemical nature

During electrolysis of $\text{LiH},\text{Ca}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$, etc, in molten state hydrogen is evolved at the anode indicating its electronegative nature. In this respect, hydrogen shows resemblance with halogens which are also liberated at the anode during electrolysis.

4. Oxidation state

Just like halogens, hydrogen also exhibit state of –1 in some of its compounds such as metal hydrides.

$\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+1}}{{\text{H}}^{-1}}\quad \text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}$

5. Combination with alkali metals

Just like halogen, hydrogen also combines with alkali metals to form salts with similar formulae.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{NaH} & \text{LiH} & \text{Ca}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \\ \text{NaCl} & \text{LiCl} & \text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \\ \end{array}$

6. Combination with non-metals

Just like halogens hydrogen also react with non-metals such as carbon, silicon, germanium, etc, to form covalent compounds.

Halides: $\quad \text{CC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}\quad \text{SiC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}\quad \text{GeC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$

Hydrides: $\quad \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\quad \text{Si}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\quad \text{Ge}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$

7. Ionization energy

Ionization energy of hydrogen is comparable to the ionization energies of halogens as shown below:

$\begin{matrix} \text{ Element :}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{ } & \text{H} & \text{F} & \text{Cl} & \text{Br} \\ \text{ Ionization energies }\left( \text{kJmo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}} \right): & 1312 & 1681 & 1255 & 1121 \\ \end{matrix}$

concepts.title: Isotope of Hydrogen
concepts.conceptBody:
Isotopes of Hydrogen

It has been found by mass spectrograph that hydrogen has three isotopes namely; protium, deuterium and tritium. The relative abudance of three isotopes of hydrogen is as under.

$\begin{matrix} \text{Isotopes } & _{1}^{1}\text{H} & : & _{1}^{2}\text{H} & : & _{1}^{3}\text{H} \\ \text{Abudance} & 1 & : & 1.5\times {{10}^{-2}} & : & 1\times {{10}^{-17}} \\ \end{matrix}$

(a) Protium or hydrogen

It is represented by the symbol$\text{H}$. Its atomic number is 1 and mass number is also 1 . It has one proton (but no neutron) in its nucleus and one electron in its 1s orbital. Naturally occurring hydrogen contains$99.985%$of this isotopes.

(b) Deuterium or heavy hydrogen

It is represented by the symbol D or$_{1}^{2}\text{H}$. It's atomic number is 1 and mass number is 2 . It has proton and one neutron in its nucleus and one electron in its 1 s orbital. Naturally occurring hydrogen has $0.15%$ of this isotopes mostly in the form of HD.

(c) Tritium

It is represented by the symbol of $\text{T}$ or $_{1}^{3}\text{H}$. Its nucleus has one proton and 2 neutron and there is one electron in its 1 s orbital. It is an extremely rare isotope. Out of ${{10}^{17}}$ molecules of ordinary hydrogen there is just one molecule of tritium. this isotope of hydrogen is radioactive in nature and emits low energy $\beta $-particles $\left( {{t}_{1/2}}=12.33\gamma \right)$.

It may be noted that three isotopes of hydrogen have properties because of the same electronic configuration, $1~{{\text{s}}^{1}}$. However due to different mass numbers they have different rates of chemical reactions. For example, reaction between protium and chlorine is 13.4 times faster than that between deuterium and chlorine. Similarly, electrolysis of ordinary water $\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)$ occurs more rapidly than of heavy water $\left( {{\text{D}}_{2}}\text{O} \right)$. Difference in properties arising due to the difference in mass number is referred to as isotopic effect .

(d) Ortho and para hydrogen

When the spins of the nuclei are in the same direction (parallel spins), dihydrogen is called ortho hydrogen and when the spins are in the opposite direction (anti parallel spins), dihydrogen is called para hydrogen.
